finding.vuln_claim: Unvalidated `dir` is interpolated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause, enabling SQL injection via crafted values.
The endpoint accepts attacker-controlled `req.query.url` and performs `axios.get(u)`, enabling SSRF and potential internal data exfiltration via `res.send(data.data)`.
The endpoint directly assigns request-supplied `note` to `inv.note` without validation or sanitization, enabling malicious content to be persisted.
Missing handling for a non-existent invoice can cause a server error when attempting to access `inv.note`, enabling denial-of-service.
User-controlled `req.query.file` is only naively sanitized and then joined to a server path, enabling path traversal to read files outside `/srv/data`.
The endpoint blindly deserializes attacker-controlled cookie data with `pickle.loads`, enabling remote code execution.
The authorization condition is malformed/ambiguous, so the endpoint may allow updating any user without proper permission checks.
The handler mass-assigns request body fields into the user model, enabling overwriting sensitive properties.
Untrusted request.json fields are interpolated into a shell command executed via os.system, enabling command injection.
User-controlled `body` is converted to HTML via `markdown.markdown` and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS when rendered later.
The endpoint allows negative `amount` values because it casts with `int()` and only checks `if src < amt`, enabling balance manipulation.
Debit and credit are performed as two separate operations without any transaction/locking, allowing concurrent requests to create inconsistent balances (double-spend).
In production, failed signature verification falls back to `jwt.decode(t)`, which performs no cryptographic verification, enabling forged/invalid tokens to be treated as authenticated.
`login_required` authenticates but there is no authorization check that restricts which `uid` a user may look up, enabling object-level data leakage.
The HMAC signature check uses a non-constant-time equality comparison (`got == want`), enabling a timing side-channel to gradually guess a valid `X-Sig`.
User-controlled `next` is only validated by `startswith('/')`, so a scheme-relative value like `//attacker.example` bypasses the check and causes an off-site redirect.
User-controlled XML is parsed with external entity resolution enabled, allowing SSRF/file disclosure via XXE.
User-supplied `url` is only checked for `startswith('http')`, enabling SSRF to internal hosts/ports.
The code downloads the entire response body into memory with no size limit, enabling resource exhaustion DoS via large responses.
Use of `gets(buf)` allows unbounded input to overflow the 64-byte stack buffer.
`saveToken` stores the access token in app SharedPreferences in plaintext, without encryption or a secure keystore.
The AES key is hardcoded as a constant byte string, enabling key extraction and full decryption of all ciphertexts.
Ciphertext provides no integrity/authentication (AES-CBC with padding only), so it is malleable and can be tampered with to alter plaintext.
The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` is inverted, so a NULL `out` pointer passes and is dereferenced by `memcpy`/`out[n]='\0'`.
User-controlled `dir` is inserted into an SQL `ORDER BY` clause without validation/parameterization, enabling SQL injection.
`/fetch` accepts attacker-controlled `req.query.url` and blindly requests it with `axios.get`, enabling SSRF to internal services.
The proxy returns arbitrary remote response content directly to the client, which can cause reflected XSS when the fetched content is script-capable HTML.
update_invoice calls request.json.get(...) without handling missing or invalid JSON, which can raise an exception and enable a denial-of-service.
User-controlled `file` enables path traversal / arbitrary file read via insufficient sanitization before `path.join` and `fs.readFile`.
The handler deserializes attacker-controlled cookie `state` with `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via crafted pickle payloads.
The handler mass-assigns all fields from req.body onto the user object via Object.assign, enabling unauthorized field changes.
The authorization check appears incorrect because it does not clearly compare req.user.id with the target id before allowing the update.
Unvalidated user-controlled `name` is interpolated into a shell command executed by `os.system`, enabling command injection.
Although `src` is checked for a prefix, it is still interpolated unquoted into a shell command, allowing injection via shell metacharacters after the allowed prefix.
User-controlled `body` is converted to HTML via Markdown without sanitization, enabling stored XSS when rendered later.
`GET /comments` returns all comments without any authorization filtering, risking data exposure across users.
`amount` is not validated for positivity, so a negative transfer amount can increase the sender balance and debit the recipient.
Balance updates are not performed atomically, so concurrent requests can cause double-spend or inconsistent balances.
On JWT verification failure in production, the code falls back to `jwt.decode(t)` which does not validate signatures, enabling authentication bypass.
Access control is enforced only for nonzero IDs, allowing an authenticated user to retrieve the email for user id=0 (potential IDOR).
The signature check uses `got == want`, enabling a timing side-channel that can leak the correct HMAC value byte-by-byte.
The handler reads and processes the full request body without any size limit, allowing resource exhaustion during HMAC and JSON parsing.
The '/go' endpoint performs an untrusted redirect based on the user-controlled 'next' parameter, allowing scheme-relative off-site redirects.
The endpoint parses attacker-controlled XML with external entity resolution enabled (resolve_entities=True) and network access allowed (no_network=False), enabling XXE.
User-controlled `req.body` is used to build the database query object, enabling potential NoSQL injection via operator injection in the `email`/`password` fields.
The login decision branch is effectively unconditional because the condition is `if ( true )`, risking authentication bypass depending on what follows inside the branch.
Use of `gets(buf)` enables an unbounded stack buffer overflow in `main`.
The code stores an access token in plaintext SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling token theft.
The AES key is hardcoded as a static constant, so compromise or code access exposes all past and future ciphertexts.
AES-CBC is used without any integrity/authentication, enabling ciphertext malleability and potential padding-oracle style exploits.
The NULL-check is incorrect (`if (out || out_sz==0)`), so passing `out=NULL` with `out_sz>0` leads to dereferencing `out` in `memcpy`/assignment.
User-controlled `dir` is interpolated into an SQL `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.
The route fetches an attacker-controlled `url` via `axios.get` and returns the result, enabling SSRF to internal network resources.
update_invoice does not handle db.get_invoice(inv_id) returning None before accessing inv.project_id, which can trigger a server error for invalid invoice IDs.
User-controlled `req.query.file` is only sanitized with a single `replace('../','')`, allowing path traversal and arbitrary file reads under/above `/srv/data`.
Untrusted user-controlled cookie is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling remote code execution via malicious pickle payloads.
Mass-assignment allows updating sensitive user fields from req.body without field-level allowlisting.
User-controlled `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed via `os.system`, enabling command injection.
User-supplied `body` is converted to HTML via `markdown.markdown()` and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS when rendered in clients.
`amount` is not validated for sign, so a negative value passes the insufficient-funds check and reverses balance effects.
Balance updates are not performed atomically, enabling inconsistent state under concurrent transfers.
In production, verification errors fall back to jwt.decode(t), which performs no signature/claim validation, enabling authentication bypass with forged tokens.
The authorization check uses `if uid:` so nonzero `id` is forbidden, but `id=0` bypasses the 403 and proceeds to the user lookup.
The signature check uses `got == want`, which is not constant-time and may leak information via response timing differences.
Open redirect due to insufficient validation of the user-controlled `next` parameter allowing scheme-relative external redirects.
The endpoint enables XXE by using XMLParser(resolve_entities=True) and parsing untrusted request.data with etree.fromstring.
User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side with only a `startswith('http')` check, enabling SSRF to internal services.
Server downloads full response body into memory via `.content`, allowing memory/CPU DoS with large payloads.
Use of `gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling stack overflow.
Sensitive auth token is persisted in plain SharedPreferences without encryption or secure storage.
The AES key is hardcoded as a constant byte string, enabling anyone with code access to decrypt ciphertexts.
AES-CBC encryption is provided without integrity/authentication, making the ciphertext malleable and potentially vulnerable to padding-oracle style attacks.
The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` is inverted, so a NULL `out` can slip through and cause NULL-pointer dereference in `memcpy`/write.
`dir` from `request.args` is interpolated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.
Unvalidated `req.query.url` is fetched by `axios.get` and returned to the client, enabling SSRF to arbitrary URLs (and the malformed guard on line 3).
The handler assumes `request.json` is always present and valid, so malformed or missing JSON can trigger an uncaught exception.
User-controlled `note` is stored without any sanitization/validation, enabling stored XSS if the note is later rendered unsafely.
Path traversal/LFI occurs because user input is insufficiently sanitized and used to construct a server path for fs.readFile.
Untrusted cookie content is base64-decoded and deserialized with pickle.loads, enabling attacker-controlled code execution.
Authorization logic appears malformed/incorrect, potentially allowing unauthorized PATCH of any user by id.
Mass assignment via `Object.assign(user, req.body)` allows updating sensitive fields included in the request body.
User-controlled `name` is interpolated into a shell command executed via `os.system`, enabling OS command injection.
User-controlled `src` is only prefix-checked and then unquoted into a shell command, allowing injection using payloads that still start with `/var/data/`.
User-controlled `body` is converted to HTML via `markdown.markdown()` and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS if the HTML is later rendered.
In `production`, failed `jwt.verify` errors are suppressed and `jwt.decode` is returned, enabling forged tokens to be treated as valid auth data without signature verification.
The production fallback uses `jwt.decode` which does not enforce time-based/claim validations (e.g., `exp`, `nbf`, `aud`, `iss`) that `jwt.verify` would typically validate.
The authorization check is inverted (`if uid: return forbidden`), so only `id=0` bypasses the 403 and reaches the user lookup query.
The webhook verifies HMAC using `got == want`, enabling timing side-channel leakage instead of constant-time comparison.
Open redirect persists because `next` allows network-path references starting with `//`, letting browsers interpret it as an external origin.
XXE allows attacker-controlled external entities to be resolved, enabling SSRF or file disclosure via XML parsing.
User-controlled `email`/`password` are placed directly into the database query without validation or operator sanitization, enabling NoSQL query/operator injection to bypass auth.
The authentication logic appears to use a hardcoded `if ( true )`, which would make the login path unconditional if the block contains success handling.
User-controlled `url` is only checked for `http` prefix, enabling SSRF to internal hosts and metadata services via `requests.get(url)`.
Unbounded download of `requests.get(url).content` can cause resource exhaustion (memory/CPU) when fetching very large responses.
Use of `gets(buf)` allows unbounded input to overflow the 64-byte stack buffer `buf`.
`saveToken()` stores an access token in plaintext `SharedPreferences`, enabling token theft if the device data is accessed.
The encryption key is hardcoded in source code, enabling key extraction and compromise across all deployments.
AES-CBC encryption is used without any integrity/authentication (e.g., MAC/AEAD), allowing ciphertext malleability and undetected tampering.
The NULL-check is inverted (checks `out` instead of `!out`), allowing a NULL `out` to reach `memcpy`/`out[n]`.
`direction` from `request.args['dir']` is concatenated into raw SQL without validation/parameterization, enabling SQL injection.
Unvalidated `req.query.url` is fetched server-side, enabling SSRF to internal hosts/services.
User-controlled `req.query.file` is only partially sanitized and can traverse directories, enabling arbitrary file reads from `/srv/data`.
Insecure pickle deserialization of attacker-controlled cookie data can lead to arbitrary code execution.
Mass assignment in user update lets an authenticated user set protected fields (e.g., isSupport/admin) via req.body.
User-controlled `name` and partially-validated `src` are interpolated into a shell command executed via `os.system`, enabling OS command injection.
Untrusted `body` is converted to HTML via `markdown.markdown` and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS if the markdown renderer allows raw HTML.
The handler casts user-supplied amount to int and only checks src<amt, so negative values bypass the check and invert the debit/credit logic.
Balance validation and updates are non-atomic, enabling race conditions where concurrent requests can overdraw before updates are applied.
In production, verification failures fall back to jwt.decode(t), which does not verify signatures, enabling authentication bypass.
Authorization logic appears inverted, returning 403 when `id` is truthy and allowing lookups only when `id=0`.
Signature verification uses non-constant-time comparison (`got == want`), enabling timing side-channel leaks.
No request body size limits or input validation before `json.loads(body)`, allowing CPU/memory DoS via oversized or malformed JSON.
Unvalidated `next` allows an attacker to trigger an open redirect to an external host via scheme-relative URLs like `//evil.com`.
XML is parsed with resolve_entities=True and no_network=False, enabling XXE via external entities during fromstring.
Authentication logic uses a constant `if ( true )`, which can cause login to succeed without verifying the user/password.
User lookup builds a query object directly from `req.body`, enabling NoSQL/ORM query operator injection and auth bypass.
The program uses `gets(buf)` on a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling a stack-based buffer overflow.
The access token is stored in plaintext in SharedPreferences via putString, enabling offline theft if device storage is accessed.
The AES key is hardcoded in source, enabling offline compromise if the file is leaked or recovered from binaries.
AES-CBC encryption is used without authentication/integrity checks, making ciphertext malleable and enabling undetected plaintext tampering.
NULL pointer check is inverted (`if (out || out_sz==0)`), so `out==NULL` with `out_sz>0` reaches `memcpy`/write and dereferences NULL.
Unvalidated `dir` is interpolated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause, enabling SQL injection.
Unvalidated user-controlled `url` is fetched server-side, enabling SSRF to internal hosts.
The server proxies arbitrary remote content directly to the client, which can become an XSS vector if rendered as HTML in the browser.
`note` is taken from `request.json` and saved without validation/sanitization, enabling stored XSS if later rendered unsafely.
If `db.get_invoice(inv_id)` returns `None`, `inv.project_id` access can crash the handler, causing a DoS and potential error info leak.
`req.query.file` is insufficiently sanitized, allowing path traversal that can make `path.join('/srv/data', f)` point outside the intended directory before `fs.readFile`.
The endpoint deserializes attacker-controlled data from the `state` cookie using `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution.
The authorization guard at line 2 appears incorrect/insufficient, while line 5 loads the target user by attacker-controlled `:id`, enabling IDOR-style updates.
`Object.assign(user, req.body)` allows mass assignment of sensitive fields, enabling privilege escalation through unexpected properties.
Untrusted `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed via `os.system`, enabling command injection.
`name` is used to construct the output path `/tmp/{name}.tgz` without validation, allowing path traversal/safe-write bypass.
create_comment renders user-supplied markdown to HTML and stores it without sanitization, enabling stored XSS when the html is later rendered.
`amount` is not validated to be positive, so a negative transfer amount can increase the sender balance and decrease the recipient balance.
The debit and credit operations are not shown as an atomic transaction, enabling race-condition/double-spend inconsistencies under concurrent requests.
In production, failed jwt.verify falls back to jwt.decode(t), which does not verify the signature, enabling token forgery/auth bypass.
An authenticated user can query arbitrary user records (including email) by providing an `id`, without verifying authorization for that specific object.
Signature check uses `got == want`, leaking timing differences that can help an attacker gradually guess a valid HMAC.
HMAC validation signs only the request body, with no nonce/timestamp, enabling replay of previously captured valid webhook calls.
User-controlled `next` permits scheme-relative redirect targets like `//attacker.com`, enabling open redirect to an external domain.
XXE is enabled by resolve_entities=True and parsing attacker-supplied XML with fromstring(request.data).
External entities can reach the network because no_network=False, enabling SSRF via XXE.
Unvalidated user input is used directly in the database query object, enabling NoSQL/operator injection.
Authentication/authorization check is effectively always true, allowing login without valid credentials.
Unrestricted `requests.get(url)` fetches attacker-supplied HTTP(S) URLs, enabling SSRF to internal hosts.
It loads the full HTTP response into memory (`requests.get(...).content`) with no body size limit, enabling DoS.
Use of `gets(buf)` allows unbounded input to overflow `buf[64]`, corrupting stack control data.
The code persists an access token to SharedPreferences without encryption, making it recoverable from compromised devices.
Hardcoded AES key is embedded in the source, enabling anyone with repo access to decrypt all ciphertext.
AES-CBC encryption is returned without any integrity/authentication, allowing ciphertext tampering and padding-oracle style attacks.
